reason: While the text begins by listing Jacob and his sons (the patriarchs/leaders) by name, it immediately shifts focus to the collective experience of 'the children of Israel' as a unified people. The subsequent narrative describes the Egyptian oppression and enslavement as affecting all the Israelites equally, without any distinction suggesting that leaders held greater importance or special treatment compared to regular people. The passage emphasizes the collective growth ('the people multiplied exceedingly'), shared oppression ('they enslaved the children of Israel'), and the common suffering of the entire nation. Moses is introduced as an individual born into this collective tragedy, not yet in a leadership role. The text establishes genealogy and naming conventions rather than a hierarchy of importance.
